revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fa:جنبش براندازی شوگون]][[ja:倒幕運動]][[ko:도막운동]][[zh:倒幕運動]]
'''Tōbaku Undō''' (倒幕運動) refers to political movements or activities aimed at overthrowing the {{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|qid=Q131767|lt=Shogun|1=Shogun}} (幕府). The term is primarily used to describe the political movements of the late {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}} aimed at overthrowing the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and establishing a new government. In a narrow sense, it refers specifically to armed movements against the bakufu, while in a broader sense, it also encompasses revolutionary movements that sought to minimize or avoid military conflict during the transfer of power. The term "Tōbaku Undō" also applies to the movements led by {{ill|Emperor Go-Daigo|en|Emperor Go-Daigo|qid=Q313942|lt=Emperor Go-Daigo|1=Emperor Go-Daigo}} at the end of the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|qid=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}}, such as the [[Shōchū Incident]] and the {{ill|Genkō War|en|Genkō War|qid=Q1432624|lt=Genkō War|1=Genkō War}}, aimed at overthrowing the Kamakura shogunate. In contrast, during the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|qid=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}, {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|qid=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga|1=Oda Nobunaga}} ousted {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshiaki|en|Ashikaga Yoshiaki|qid=Q434415|lt=Ashikaga Yoshiaki|1=Ashikaga Yoshiaki}} without officially dismissing him from his position as {{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|qid=Q131767|lt=Shogun|1=Shogun}}, and Yoshiaki continued to be recognized as shōgun by families like the {{ill|Mōri clan|en|Mōri clan|qid=Q914637|lt=Mōri clan|1=Mōri clan}}. When Yoshiaki became a monk under the [[Toyotomi administration]], he was considered to have lost his shōgun title automatically (as noted in the ''[[Kuge Bukan]]''), and no "Tōbaku Undō" against the [[:en:Muromachi shogunate|Muromachi shogunate]] occurred. == Overview == {{Citation needed|date=April 2024|section=1|qq=}} During the Edo period, the development of {{ill|Kokugaku|en|Kokugaku|qid=Q855159|lt=Kokugaku|1=Kokugaku}} ([[Japanese classical studies]]) led to criticism of samurai governments and a revival of [[Ōsei Fukko]] (restoration of imperial rule). Simultaneously, frequent visits by foreign ships increased pressure on Japan. In {{ill|1853|en|1853|qid=Q7666|lt=1853|1=1853}} (the 6th year of {{ill|Kaei|en|Kaei|qid=Q1146125|lt=Kaei|1=Kaei}}), the {{ill|Black Ships|en|Black Ships|qid=Q1136943|lt=Black Ships|1=Black Ships}} of {{ill|Matthew Perry|en|Matthew Perry|qid=Q171758|lt=Matthew Perry|1=Matthew Perry}} from the {{ill|United States|en|United States|qid=Q30|lt=United States|1=United States}} and {{ill|Yevfimiy Putyatin|en|Yevfimiy Putyatin|qid=Q957601|lt=Yevfimiy Putyatin|1=Yevfimiy Putyatin}} from the {{ill|Russian Empire|en|Russian Empire|qid=Q34266|lt=Russian Empire|1=Russian Empire}} arrived, demanding {{ill|Trade|en|Trade|qid=Q601401|lt=Trade|1=Trade}} agreements. By {{ill|1858|en|1858|qid=Q7684|lt=1858|1=1858}} (the 5th year of {{ill|Ansei|en|Ansei|qid=Q1327259|lt=Ansei|1=Ansei}}), the Tokugawa shogunate signed the {{ill|Ansei Treaties|en|Ansei Treaties|qid=Q1152499|lt=Ansei Treaties|1=Ansei Treaties}} with multiple nations, leading to the {{ill|Opening of Japan|en|Opening of Japan|qid=Q11655196|lt=Opening of Japan|1=Opening of Japan}}. However, at the time, the {{ill|Imperial Court|en|Imperial Court|qid=Q6006523|lt=Imperial Court|1=Imperial Court}} was dominated by [[:en:Jōi|Jōi]]-leaning [[kuge]] nobles, and treaties signed without imperial sanction were deemed invalid. This exacerbated tensions between the court and the shogunate, resulting in incidents such as the {{ill|Ansei Purge|en|Ansei Purge|qid=Q569487|lt=Ansei Purge|1=Ansei Purge}} and the [[:en:Sakuradamon Incident|Sakuradamon Incident]], where {{ill|Ii Naosuke|en|Ii Naosuke|qid=Q716940|lt=Ii Naosuke|1=Ii Naosuke}} was assassinated in {{ill|1860|en|1860|qid=Q7691|lt=1860|1=1860}}. The shogunate sought to restructure power through the [[:en:kōbugattai|kōbugattai]] ("Union of Court and Camp") policy and postponed opening ports through agreements such as the [[:en:London Memorandum|London Memorandum]] in {{ill|1862|en|1862|qid=Q7698|lt=1862|1=1862}} (2nd year of {{ill|Bunkyū|en|Bunkyū|qid=Q730266|lt=Bunkyū|1=Bunkyū}}). Foreign powers, however, began seeking imperial sanction for treaties, inadvertently restoring the court's influence. At the same time, {{ill|shishi|en|shishi|qid=Q1040416|lt=shishi|1=Shishi}} activists in the anti-bakufu faction, inspired by {{ill|Mitogaku|en|Mitogaku|qid=Q260529|lt=Mitogaku|1=Mitogaku}} philosophy, advocated for armed resistance. Leaders like [[Saigō Takamori]] (Kichinosuke), {{ill|Ōkubo Toshimichi|en|Ōkubo Toshimichi|qid=Q356118|lt=Ōkubo Toshimichi|1=Ōkubo Toshimichi}}, and [[Komatsu Kiyomichi]] from the {{ill|Satsuma Domain|en|Satsuma Domain|qid=Q676187|lt=Satsuma Domain|1=Satsuma Domain}}, {{ill|Kido Takayoshi|en|Kido Takayoshi|qid=Q696402|lt=Kido Takayoshi|1=Kido Takayoshi}} (Katsura Kogorō) and [[Hirozawa Masami]] from the {{ill|Chōshū Domain|en|Chōshū Domain|qid=Q876267|lt=Chōshū Domain|1=Chōshū Domain}}, [[Takechi Zuisan]] and [[Yoshimura Toratarō]] from the {{ill|Tosa Domain|en|Tosa Domain|qid=Q1189704|lt=Tosa Domain|1=Tosa Domain}}, and the [[kuge]] [[Iwakura Tomomi]] promoted [[Ōsei Fukko]] and resisted foreign influences. The {{ill|Aizu Domain|en|Aizu Domain|qid=Q4389944|lt=Aizu Domain|1=Aizu Domain}}, which supported kōbugattai, along with {{ill|Satsuma Domain|en|Satsuma Domain|qid=Q676187|lt=Satsuma Domain|1=Satsuma Domain}}, initially suppressed anti-bakufu factions in events like the [[Tenchū-gumi Incident]] and the [[:en:Kinmon Incident|Kinmon Incident]] in {{ill|1863|en|1863|qid=Q7701|lt=1863|1=1863}} (Bunkyū 3). The Chōshū Domain, designated as imperial enemies after the [[August 18 Coup]], faced [[Shimonoseki Campaigns]] and [[:en:Chōshū Expeditions|Chōshū Expeditions]], further curtailing anti-bakufu movements temporarily. Despite this, activists like {{ill|Takasugi Shinsaku|en|Takasugi Shinsaku|qid=Q700310|lt=Takasugi Shinsaku|1=Takasugi Shinsaku}} from Chōshū reorganized forces and maintained opposition. Satsuma Domain, influenced by experiences like the [[:en:Anglo-Satsuma War|Anglo-Satsuma War]], began focusing on modernization and diplomacy, culminating in the {{ill|Satchō Alliance|en|Satchō Alliance|qid=Q700896|lt=Satchō Alliance|1=Satchō Alliance}} brokered by {{ill|Sakamoto Ryōma|en|Sakamoto Ryōma|qid=Q378450|lt=Sakamoto Ryōma|1=Sakamoto Ryōma}} in {{ill|1866|en|1866|qid=Q7712|lt=1866|1=1866}}. == Key Developments == By {{ill|1867|en|1867|qid=Q7715|lt=1867|1=1867}} (Keiō 3), {{ill|Emperor Kōmei|en|Emperor Kōmei|qid=Q299179|lt=Emperor Kōmei|1=Emperor Kōmei}} had died, and {{ill|Emperor Meiji|en|Emperor Meiji|qid=Q174231|lt=Emperor Meiji|1=Emperor Meiji}} ascended the throne. Anti-bakufu factions received imperial sanction for overthrowing the shogunate. Although the 15th shogun, [[Tokugawa Yoshinobu]], attempted reconciliation through [[Taisei Hōkan]] (restoration of imperial rule), tensions escalated into the {{ill|Boshin War|en|Boshin War|qid=Q309184|lt=Boshin War|1=Boshin War}}, beginning with the {{ill|Battle of Toba–Fushimi|en|Battle of Toba–Fushimi|qid=Q1193959|lt=Battle of Toba–Fushimi|1=Battle of Toba–Fushimi}}. The [[Meiji Restoration]] formalized the dissolution of the Tokugawa shogunate and ushered in Japan's modernization.
== See Also == * [[Hōreki Incident]] - Mid-Edo period movement by the court to oppose the bakufu * [[Keichō Embassy to Europe]] - A supposed plot for foreign military alliances against the bakufu {{Japanese-history-stub|qq=}}
{{translated page|ja|倒幕運動|version=104404003|comment=Imported full ja history}}
[[ja:倒幕運動]]
